channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: In NATO’s Next Act, Can Europe Lead?
item.description: The focus at a leaders’ summit in Turkey this week will be on building a new model for the alliance, as President Trump pushes to do less and pressures others do more.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger and Lara Jakes
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: NATO leaders at last year’s summit in The Hague, where member states committed to increase spending on defense sharply by 2035.

item.title: Mass Mournings In Qom, Where Khamenei Ruled Supreme Over Iran’s Clerics
item.description: The ceremony for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, held at a site considered sacred by many Shiites, was the latest step in a weeklong funeral organized by Iran’s government.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ahmadreza Taheri/WANA News Agency, via Reuters
item.media:description: A state media handout photo is said to show mourners attending a prayer for Iran’s former supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, at the Jamkaran Mosque, in Qom, Iran, on Tuesday.

item.title: Farage Resigns and Says He Will Run Again in Special Election
item.description: Nigel Farage, leader of the populist right-wing party Reform U.K., has come under increasing pressure after a series of revelations about undisclosed gifts and donations.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:description: Nigel Farage speaks to the news media at the Havering Town Hall after the results of the local elections in the London Borough of Havering, Britain, in May.

item.title: Estonia Won the War on Fentanyl. What Came Next Was Even Worse.
item.description: By 2018, fentanyl overdoses in Estonia had plummeted. But powerful new drugs are appearing fast, with the authorities racing to respond.
item.dc:creator: Azam Ahmed and Meridith Kohut
item.media:description: Long before fentanyl swept American streets, Estonia battled the drug — and won.

item.title: Le Pen Says She Will Run for Presidency After Court Lifts Ban
item.description: An appeals court confirmed Marine Le Pen’s embezzlement conviction, but shortened a ban on her eligibility for elected office. That means the French far-right leader can run for president in 2027.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler and Ségolène Le Stradic
item.media:description: Marine Le Pen meeting with supporters at a country fair in Liévin, France, on Saturday.

item.title: Venezuelans Criticize Own Government Response After Twin Earthquakes
item.description: Venezuelans are publicly expressing their anger over their government’s disaster response, despite the risk of imprisonment.
item.dc:creator: Julie Turkewitz
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: People look for their relatives in the rubble of a public housing complex in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Friday, after twin earthquakes destroyed it.

item.title: Suspect in Monaco Bombing Attack Is Found Dead in Ukraine
item.description: The Ukrainian authorities said that Anastasiia Berezovska, who officials said was behind the explosion last week, had been shot.
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall and Stanislav Kozliuk

item.title: A Supreme Leader’s Final Crowds
item.description: What you could see when Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s coffin was being driven to a public space in Tehran.
item.dc:creator: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times

item.title: Prince Harry Loses Privacy Lawsuit Against Daily Mail Publisher
item.description: The case brought by Harry and other celebrities against Associated Newspapers was one of several legal battles that the prince has fought against British tabloids in recent years.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:description: Prince Harry arriving at a court in London, in January. He and other celebrities had accused Associated Newspapers, the publisher of the Daily Mail, of intruding into their private lives.

item.title: How a Four-Generation Cuban Family Survives on $60 a Month
item.description: The U.S. oil blockade has deepened a humanitarian crisis, forcing Cubans to lean on the island’s long tradition of community solidarity to provide a cushion.
item.dc:creator: Ed Augustin and Lisette Poole González
item.media:description: Analeidis Arias Marin gets her children ready for school in Santiago de Cuba in May.

item.title: Belgium’s World Cup Win Over U.S. Fuels Triumph and Snark in Europe
item.description: President Trump had pushed to allow an American player to take the field despite a red card suspension, and the trouncing of the U.S. team was painted as a sign of justice.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek and Koba Ryckewaert
item.media:description: Belgium fans celebrating their team’s 4-1 World Cup win against the United States in Seattle on Monday.

item.title: NATO Allies Show Trump They Are Trying to Pay Up for Defense
item.description: Some countries are making significant progress on raising their military spending. A few are stretching the definition of military spending to try to meet their targets.
item.dc:creator: Lara Jakes
item.media:description: Chancellor Friedrich Merz of Germany, second from left, on a tank at the barracks of Germany’s armed forces in Munster, Germany, in April.

item.title: Why China Fired a Long-Range Missile Into the Pacific
item.description: Beijing had long tested nuclear missiles only within its borders. A rare launch over an ocean shows it is determined to narrow the gap with the United States.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas

item.title: Macron Continues With Syria Visit Despite Explosions Near His Hotel in Damascus
item.description: President Emmanuel Macron of France signaled confidence in Syria’s leaders by staying overnight in the capital and carrying on with his visit despite blasts near his hotel.
item.dc:creator: Raja Abdulrahim and Reham Mourshed

item.title: A Look Back at Trump’s Broadsides Against NATO Allies
item.description: President Trump has rarely missed an opportunity to castigate the Western military alliance, whose leaders he’s meeting this week, as weak and ineffective.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has consistently questioned one of NATO’s core provisions: the commitment laid out in Article 5 of its founding charter to treat an attack on one member as an attack on all.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Steven Erlanger

item.title: Oil Prices Jump After U.S. and Iran Trade Strikes in Persian Gulf
item.description: Attacks on ships in the Strait of Hormuz sparked a new cycle of retaliation that could throttle the flow of energy from the region.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Hari Raj and Eric Schmitt

item.title: Germans Don’t Love Budweiser. It Won’t Take No for an Answer.
item.description: Not for the first time, Budweiser is trying to make it in Germany. But this is a tough time to sell German drinkers on the image and taste of American beer.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.media:description: A box of Anheuser-Busch Bud beer at a supermarket in Berlin.

item.title: Trump’s Arrival in Turkey for NATO Summit Adds Drama and Spectacle
item.description: The second President Trump landed in Turkey, the center of gravity shifted right to where he likes it best: himself.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh and Tyler Pager
item.media:description: President Trump with President Recep Tayyip Erdogan of Turkey after arriving in Ankara on Tuesday.

item.title: What to Know About Australia’s Contest With China in the Pacific After Beijing Tested a Missile
item.description: With a series of recent treaties and alliances, Canberra is seeking to prevent Beijing from establishing a military foothold in the Pacific Ocean.
item.dc:creator: Victoria Kim
item.media:description: Prime Minister Anthony Albanese of Australia, left, and his counterpart from Fiji, Sitiveni Rabuka, signed two security agreements, in Suva, Fiji, on Monday.

item.title: India Calls Out Meta Over Reported Child Sexual Abuse Imagery in Ads
item.description: The tech giant that owns Instagram, Facebook and WhatsApp says it is in touch with the Indian authorities about reports that exploitative content is being hawked on one of its platforms.
item.dc:creator: Zane Irwin
item.media:description: A Meta logo at a conference in Mumbai, India.

item.title: As Israel Loses Support in the U.S., Rahm Emanuel Criticizes Netanyahu
item.description: The former Chicago mayor, a Democrat exploring a 2028 presidential run, is in Tel Aviv calling for an end to unconditional U.S. support of Israel.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rahm Emanuel, the former Chicago mayor and White House chief of staff, is trying to chart a course between the anti-Israel left and the pro-Israel right.

item.title: U.S. Strikes Iran and Reimposes Sanctions After Tanker Attacks
item.description: The military operation came hours after the U.S. Treasury revoked a waiver allowing global sales of Iranian oil.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Negotiations between Iran and the United States have been paused until after multiday funeral ceremonies in Tehran for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the supreme leader who was killed on the first day of the war.

item.title: Zimbabwe’s President Signs Law Extending His Term
item.description: The measure will allow the president, Emmerson Mnangagwa, to stay in office until 2030. Critics say the move tightens his autocratic grip on power and erodes the nation’s democracy.
item.dc:creator: John Eligon
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Emmerson Mnangagwa of Zimbabwe at the United Nations headquarters in New York City in 2022.

item.title: A Plan to Stop the Far Right
item.description: The German chancellor and his center-left coalition partners are trying to hold off populist challengers. And they have a road map.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:description: Germany’s chancellor, Friedrich Merz, and his coalition partners are trying to hold off far-right challengers.

item.title: Marine Le Pen’s Court Battle Is Latest Clash Between Populists and Watchdogs
item.description: The far-right leader’s battle with the French judiciary evokes standoffs across the populist world, including ones with President Trump and Nigel Farage.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:description: Marine Le Pen leaving court in Paris on Tuesday.

item.title: The Symbols We Saw at Khamenei’s Funeral
item.description: Abdi Latif Dahir, a Middle East correspondent for The Times, explains the messages behind recurring symbols at the dayslong funeral for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir, Michael Anthony Adams, Mikhail Galustov, Rebecca Suner, Jon Hazell, Joey Sendaydiego and Rafaela Balster
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: I.O.C. Lifts Russia’s Olympic Suspension, Clearing the Way for 2028
item.description: The International Olympic Committee “provisionally” removed the ban imposed in relation to Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. A previous ban for systematic doping had already ended.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: The Russian Olympic Committee building in Moscow on Tuesday.

item.title: Man Arrested for Painting ‘Pam the Bird’ Graffiti on Bridge Tower in Melbourne
item.description: An hourslong standoff with the police ended after the man descended the 140-foot tower and surrendered. The graffiti resembled the “Pam the Bird” symbol which began appearing across the city in 2023.
item.dc:creator: Natan Odenheimer
item.media:credit: Joel Carrett/Australian Associated Press, via Reuters
item.media:description: A man scaled a 140-foot concrete Melbourne bridge tower, abseiling down its side to paint a massive cartoon bird.

item.title: Farage Says He Will Resign From UK’s Parliament, Setting Off a By-Election
item.description: Nigel Farage, leader of the populist right-wing party Reform U.K., has come under increasing pressure after a series of revelations about undisclosed gifts and donations.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle

item.title: As Messi Dominates the World Cup, a War Is Waged Over Maradona’s Image
item.description: A multimillion-dollar battle for a soccer god’s brand is being waged across continents, burning investors and tearing his family apart.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja and Lucía Cholakian Herrera
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Portraits of Argentina’s Lionel Messi and Diego Maradona at a match in Dallas last month.

item.title: Mysterious Spheres Found in Australia Are Likely Space Debris
item.description: The discovery bemused residents of a small town in northeastern Australia. The objects appear to be junk from a space launch, the country’s space agency said.
item.dc:creator: Hari Raj

item.title: South Korea Strengthens Law to Curb Spread of Fake News
item.description: An amendment to South Korea’s communications act will clamp down on purveyors of false information online. Critics worry it could threaten freedom of speech.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: Following decades of authoritarian rule, South Korea transitioned into a democracy in the late 1980s, with one of Asia’s most open media landscapes. But government officials are worried about social harm from fake news.

item.title: Iran’s Strikes on Ships in Strait of Hormuz Threaten Oil Flow
item.description: The strikes by Iran and the attacks and re-imposition of sanctions by the U.S. came weeks after the countries agreed to try to find a lasting end to their hostilities.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vessels anchored near Muscat, Oman, last month.

item.title: At Least 17 Dead in China After Flooding and Tornadoes During Typhoon Maysak
item.description: Videos showed inundated roads, farmland and buildings, and floodwaters rushing toward homes, while the region braced for a potential super typhoon this week.
item.dc:creator: Lily Kuo and Pei-Lin Wu
item.media:description: A flooded riverside near a wharf on the Yongjiang river in Nanning, in China’s southern Guangxi Province, on Monday.

item.title: What Our Reporter Saw in Tehran: A Human Tide of Mourners
item.description: After days of public mourning ceremonies in the Iranian capital, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s coffin arrived in Tehran’s Azadi Square. This is what I saw covering the miles-long procession on Monday.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir and Emile Ducke
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Crowds gathered around the coffin of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in Tehran, Iran, on Monday.

item.title: Trump Wanted a U.S. Soccer Star to Play in the World Cup. FIFA Found a Way.
item.description: There was nothing unusual about a call from President Trump or the reversal of Folarin Balogun’s suspension, said the president of FIFA, Gianni Infantino.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: FIFA’s president, Gianni Infantino, speaking to President Trump during an Oval Office visit in November.

item.title: Spate of Shark Attacks Means More Drones for Australia’s Beaches
item.description: The country is stepping up measures to keep swimmers and surfers safe after a teacher was mauled by a great white shark at Coogee Beach.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung
item.media:credit: Matthew Abbott for The New York Times

item.title: NATO Needs Turkey
item.description: The war in Ukraine and Trump’s disregard for the alliance have generated a new appreciation of what Turkey brings to the table.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.media:description: NATO opens a two-day summit in Ankara, Turkey, today.

item.title: Canada Bets on NATO Partners for Its Expanded Submarine Fleet
item.description: In selecting a joint bid from Germany and Norway, Prime Minister Mark Carney is elevating Canada’s naval power and reducing its military and economic dependency on the United States.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:description: Naval officers from Canada touring a South Korean KSS-III submarine in Esquimalt, British Columbia, in May.

item.title: Iranians Mourn a Slain Leader, and Call for Vengeance
item.description: Million of people filled the streets of Iran to bid farewell to Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, who was killed in the war with the United States and Israel.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir, Aaron Boxerman, Farnaz Fassihi and Leo Sands
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Crowds gathering around Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s casket in Tehran on Monday. The supreme leader’s body will be taken to several cities before being buried in Mashhad, Iran.

item.title: Will Prince Harry’s UK Visit Repair or Deepen the Royal Rift?
item.description: Harry arrived in London Monday night for a visit that has already been overshadowed by drama and confusion over his plans.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:description: Prince Harry and his wife, Meghan, visiting Sydney, Australia, in April. It is unclear whether Meghan and their two children will join Harry on his trip to Britain this week.

item.title: Heat Waves Are Taking a Big Toll on Chickens
item.description: The birds are particularly vulnerable to high temperatures, and France’s torrid June took a heavy toll.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan and Léontine Gallois
item.media:credit: Samuel Aranda for The New York Times

item.title: Turkey Hosts NATO Summit While Cracking Down on Critics
item.description: In the weeks leading up to the two-day event, the Turkish authorities blocked websites, jailed scores of people over allegations of terrorism and arrested a prominent comedian.
item.dc:creator: Ben Hubbard and Safak Timur
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Protesters demanding the release of Deniz Goktas, a comedian who was arrested after making jokes about President Recep Tayyip Erdogan of Turkey.

item.title: At Khamenei’s funeral, mourners call for revenge.
item.description: Mourners
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.media:description: Signs calling for revenge against President Trump were held up by people attending the funeral procession on Monday.

item.title: Families of Iranians Killed by Government Dismayed by Leader’s Funeral Pageantry
item.description: Critics of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s brutal rule have been angered by the scale of the commemorations being held to honor his life.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi and Rozhin Rezai
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Mourners attending the funeral procession of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei on Monday surround a monument in Tehran.

item.title: Iran to Bury Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in Holy City of Mashhad
item.description: The city of three million people is home to a gold-domed shrine that Shiite Muslims revere as Iran’s most sacred site. The final ceremony will take place there on Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Mashhad is Iran’s second most populous city and where Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was born.

item.title: Riot in Sri Lanka’s Negombo prison leaves at least 23 dead
item.description: The country has faced criticism from human rights activists for the conditions in its prisons, including overcrowding and failing to protect inmates.
item.dc:creator: Pamodi Waravita and Jeremy W. Peters
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Ishara S. Kodikara/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Personnel from Sri Lanka’s Special Task Force outside the Negombo prison near Colombo on Monday, following clashes between inmates and guards.

item.title: Wildfires Force Evacuation of Thousands in Southwest France
item.description: The blaze in the Pyrenees, a region bordering Spain, has scorched more than 11,000 acres so far.
item.dc:creator: Natan Odenheimer
item.media:description: A firefighting jet battling the flames in Ille-sur-Têt, France, on Monday.

item.title: Former Syrian Officials Found Guilty in Torture of Pro-Democracy Protesters
item.description: The case against the two men, who have been living in Austria since 2015, was one of the first that the country has brought under the principle of universal jurisdiction.
item.dc:creator: Carlotta Gall and Hussam Hammoud
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Marylise Vigneau for The New York Times
item.media:description: Khaled al-Halabi, a former intelligence commander under the dictator Bashar al-Assad, after a hearing in Vienna last month. Mr. al-Halabi was convicted of torture among other charges.

item.title: Khamenei’s Burial Was Delayed, Breaking With Islamic Custom
item.description: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s remains were preserved for months, according to Iran’s government. Some Islamic scholars say it was a political and security calculation.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: Iran held a memorial for Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in May, but his remains have not been interred despite the Islamic custom of burying people soon after death.

item.title: Sites That Report Venezuela’s Missing Rise Far From Quake Zone
item.description: Despite problems like duplicate submissions, tallies gathered by the websites have been cited by news organizations and critics of the Venezuelan government’s disaster response.
item.dc:creator: Ishani Desai
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Volunteers, relatives and rescue workers search for earthquake survivors under a collapsed public housing complex in Caraballeda, Venezuela, last month, The disaster has bared accuracy problems in how websites gather information on the missing.

item.title: The Iranian authorities go all out to stage an elaborate weeklong spectacle.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mourners at the Grand Mosalla mosque in Tehran on Saturday.

item.title: Fatigue, Tension, Joy: England Pulls a World Cup All-Nighter
item.description: The game against Mexico started at 2 a.m. in Britain. Pubs were allowed to stay open unusually late for fans to share the drama together.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg and Mary Turner
item.media:credit: Mary Turner for The New York Times
